1.errorText: Amber calls her friend William about a job agency called Riverside, which is based in Docklands at 497 Eastside. The agent he deals with is called Becky Jamieson. William says not to call Becky in the evening as she's always busy then trying to fill last-minute vacancies. The agency mostly recruits permanent staff for the finance sector. William says what matters most for receptionist work is communication. Temporary jobs usually last about a day at first, though they might get extended. The hourly rate is about £11. Amber should dress smartly and will need to bring her driving licence to the interview. They also ask questions about personality.

pragmatic.quote: There's the question of which page of the newspaper the item appears on — that shows how important the editor thinks the story is.

pragmatic.quote: What structure is it given? Bear in mind that not many people read beyond the first paragraph, so what has the journalist put at the beginning?
pragmatic.speaker: Tom

pragmatic.quote: Often the writer's purpose is not to inform the public — it may be to create fear, or controversy, or to make somebody look ridiculous.
pragmatic.speaker: Tom

pragmatic.quote: The writer may also make assumptions about the reader — about their knowledge of the subject, their attitudes, and their level of education.

signpost.quote: During the excavation of ancient Babylon, evidence was found that soapmaking was known as early as 2800 BC. Archaeologists discovered cylinders made of clay, with inscriptions saying that fats were boiled with ashes.
opts: Introducing the earliest archaeological evidence of soap production

signpost.quote: The early Greeks bathed for aesthetic reasons and apparently didn't use soap. Instead, they cleaned their bodies with blocks of sand, pumice and ashes.
opts: Describing an alternative cleansing system before introducing the Roman soap tradition

signpost.quote: The first of the famous Roman baths, supplied with water from their aqueducts, was built about 312 BC. The baths were luxurious, and bathing became very popular.
opts: Connecting Roman infrastructure to the popularisation of bathing

signpost.quote: After the fall of Rome in 467 AD and the resulting decline in bathing habits, much of Europe felt the impact of filth on public health.
opts: Linking the fall of Rome to the collapse of hygiene practices and subsequent disease

signpost.quote: A major step toward large-scale commercial soapmaking occurred in 1791, when a French chemist patented a process for turning salt into soda ash.
opts: Questioning the importance of Leblanc's discovery
Identifying a specific industrial turning point in soap history before introducing the scientific breakthrough

1.scene: 铁路乘客满意度调查访谈

mcq.q: Why is Sophie in town today?
opts: For shopping on her day off

mcq.q: What does Sophie say about the ticket office?
opts: It is open 24 hours a day
It closes early and she hates using ticket machines

mcq.q: What does Sophie say is the best improvement at the station?

1.errorText: A man conducts a customer satisfaction survey for the railway. Sophie Bird is a journalist who came to town today for a business meeting. She is travelling back to Staunfirth. She bought a regular return ticket and paid £23.70 for it. She bought the ticket online yesterday because the ticket office closes early and she hates using machines. She was most satisfied with the wifi on the train. She was not satisfied with the fact that the train was delayed — it was about 15 minutes late. She thinks the best improvement at the station is the increased number of seats. She is neither satisfied nor dissatisfied with the seating.

errors.correct: return ticket (it's just a standard return, not a special type)

errors.wrong: She is neither satisfied nor dissatisfied with the seating
errors.correct: she is dissatisfied with the lack of seating (not enough places to sit)

4.scene: 工业革命消费主义社会史讲座

signpost.quote: It was in the 19th century that the quantity and quality of people's possessions was used as an indication of the wealth of the country.
opts: Establishing the historical context in which consumerism first became measurable

signpost.quote: When it comes to manufacturing, we tend to think of the Industrial Revolution in images of steam engines and coal — and it's true that the Industrial Revolution couldn't have taken place at all if it weren't for these new sources of power.
opts: Validating a common association before broadening the discussion

signpost.quote: As well as manufacturing, there were new technologies in transport, contributing to the growth of consumerism.
opts: Signalling a shift from manufacturing to transport as the second contributing area

signpost.quote: Significantly, the goods railways moved included newspapers, which meant that thousands of people were not only more knowledgeable about what was going on, but could also read about what was available in the shops.
opts: Identifying how newspapers specifically acted as a driver of consumer behaviour

signpost.quote: The third main area that saw changes contributing to consumerism was retailing.
opts: Introducing the final key area after manufacturing and transport

of the country.

The Industrial Revolution couldn't have taken place without new sources of power. These marked an important shift away from watermills and windmills. The most advanced industry for much of the 19th century was
Before the Industrial Revolution, people made goods in small workshops. But enormous new
could produce goods faster and on a larger scale — requiring a lot more space.
Horse-drawn stagecoaches were gradually replaced by canals, and then by railways — the main way of moving goods and people. Significantly, they also moved
, which meant people could read about what was available in shops.
Faster transport made distribution more efficient — goods could now be sold all over the country instead of just in the
market.
In retailing, small shops suffered as customers flocked to department stores. Improved
inside greatly increased the visibility of goods for sale.
The use of plate glass made
much larger than previously — goods could be seen from outside.
New ways of promoting goods were introduced: previously the focus had been on informing customers about the availability of goods. Now there was an explosion in
trying to persuade people to go shopping.
Flanders claims that one of the great effects of the Industrial Revolution was that it created
. All sorts of things that had been luxuries — from sugar to cutlery — became conveniences, and before long they'd turned into necessities.
